subject: ap-psychology
title: Developmental Psychology — Piaget, Attachment, and Memory
stimulus:
Gavin's parents took him to his favorite science museum, and he explored all of the exhibits. One of the
interactive exhibits featured glass marbles. He grabbed a large marble and tried to bounce it on the ground. At
one point during the visit, he became separated from his parents, and it took a few minutes for them to find
him. Later that night, he drew a colorful picture of the exhibits at the museum. The next day, Gavin brought his
picture with him to preschool. While his grandmother drove, Gavin talked to her about the picture.

parts.prompt: Explain how Jean Piaget's concept of assimilation could relate to Gavin's experience with the glass marble.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin attempts to treat the glass marbles like "balls" by trying to bounce them on the ground.

required_elements: Gavin thought the marble would bounce like all other balls.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin thought the marble would bounce like all other balls.

wrong_examples: Gavin now has a schema for glass marbles and knows they don't bounce like balls.
Gavin thought the marbles were like bouncy balls, so he tried to bounce them. Then he changed his schema when he learned that glass marbles are different and cannot be bounced.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin attempts to treat the glass marbles like 'balls' by trying to bounce them on the ground. Responses that describe accommodation (Gavin changing or updating his schema after the experience) do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin thought the marble would bounce like all other balls.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies assimilation — Gavin fits the marble into his existing ball schema by attempting to bounce it.

sample_responses.response: Gavin now has a schema for glass marbles and knows they don't bounce like balls.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes accommodation (a schema change after the new experience), not assimilation.

sample_responses.response: Gavin thought the marbles were like bouncy balls, so he tried to bounce them. Then he changed his schema when he learned that glass marbles are different and cannot be bounced.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; the response begins with assimilation but then describes accommodation, which directly contradicts the assimilation application.

parts.prompt: Explain how egocentrism could relate to how Gavin interacted with his grandmother in the car.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin only considers his own perspective or assumes that others know what he knows when interacting with his grandmother.

required_elements: Gavin holds up the picture in a way where he is the only one who can see it, but he thinks his grandmother can see it.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin holds up the picture in a way where he is the only one who can see it, but he thinks his grandmother can see it.

required_elements: Gavin talks about the museum trip from his perspective and doesn't recognize his grandmother is busy driving.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin talks about the museum trip from his perspective and doesn't recognize his grandmother is busy driving.

wrong_examples: Gavin does not care what his grandma thinks because all he cares about is the picture he drew.
Gavin tells his grandmother all about the trip to the museum.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin only considers his own perspective or assumes others know what he knows. Responses that describe Gavin as merely selfish, or that describe him sharing information without referencing a lack of perspective-taking, do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin holds up the picture in a way where he is the only one who can see it, but he thinks his grandmother can see it.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies egocentrism — Gavin assumes his grandmother shares his visual perspective.

sample_responses.response: Gavin talks about the museum trip from his perspective and doesn't recognize his grandmother is busy driving.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies egocentrism — Gavin cannot take his grandmother's perspective (that she is occupied with driving).

sample_responses.response: Gavin does not care what his grandma thinks because all he cares about is the picture he drew.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes Gavin as selfish rather than demonstrating the cognitive inability to take another's perspective.

sample_responses.response: Gavin tells his grandmother all about the trip to the museum.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; simply describes Gavin sharing information without indicating a lack of perspective-taking.

parts.prompt: Explain how avoidant attachment could relate to Gavin when he was separated from his parents.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate a lack of distress when separated from his parents.

required_elements: Gavin didn't get upset or cry when he was separated from his parents.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin didn't get upset or cry when he was separated from his parents.

required_elements: When separated from his parents, Gavin spent the time playing by himself at different exhibits.
alternatives.correct_example: When separated from his parents, Gavin spent the time playing by himself at different exhibits.

wrong_examples: Gavin was very fearful and anxious when his parents left him.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate a lack of distress when separated from parents. Responses that describe high distress or anxiety at separation describe anxious/resistant attachment, not avoidant attachment, and do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin didn't get upset or cry when he was separated from his parents.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies the hallmark of avoidant attachment — little to no distress during separation.

sample_responses.response: When separated from his parents, Gavin spent the time playing by himself at different exhibits.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies avoidant attachment — Gavin independently explores rather than showing distress.

sample_responses.response: Gavin was very fearful and anxious when his parents left him.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes anxious/resistant attachment, not avoidant attachment.

parts.prompt: Explain how Serial position effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin recalls information from the beginning or ending of his experience better than information that is in the middle of his experience at the museum.

required_elements: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning of the day better.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning of the day better.

required_elements: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the end of the day better.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the end of the day better.

required_elements: Gavin had trouble remembering which exhibits he visited in the middle of the day.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin had trouble remembering which exhibits he visited in the middle of the day.

required_elements: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning and end of the day better than the ones in the middle of the day.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning and end of the day better than the ones in the middle of the day.

wrong_examples: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning of the day.
Gavin uses more of his motor cortex to use his fingers to draw the picture.
Gavin uses his motor cortex to grab the marble.
Gavin moves a lot when he plays.
Gavin remembered where the entrance was to the museum because of his mental map.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin recalls information from the beginning OR ending of his experience better than information from the middle. Responses that refer to primacy or recency effects but make no reference to better recall (e.g., simply 'Gavin remembered the beginning exhibits') do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning and end of the day better than the ones in the middle of the day.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies the serial position effect — better recall for items at both the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) compared to the middle.

sample_responses.response: Gavin had trouble remembering which exhibits he visited in the middle of the day.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly captures the serial position effect by identifying poorer recall for items in the middle of the sequence.

sample_responses.response: Gavin remembered the exhibits he visited at the beginning of the day.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; mentions primacy but makes no reference to better recall — the response must indicate the comparison (beginning/end better than middle).

parts.prompt: Explain how Motor cortex relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin uses his motor cortex to control voluntary movements in the scenario (e.g., grabbing the marble, drawing the picture, exploring the exhibits).

required_elements: Gavin uses his motor cortex to control his hand movements when grabbing the marble.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin uses his motor cortex to control his hand movements when grabbing the marble.

required_elements: Gavin's motor cortex is used to control the movements of his fingers as he draws his picture.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin's motor cortex is used to control the movements of his fingers as he draws his picture.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin's motor cortex controls his voluntary movements (e.g., grabbing the marble, drawing the picture). Responses that describe Gavin as simply 'moving a lot' without identifying the motor cortex's role in controlling voluntary movement do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin uses his motor cortex to grab the marble.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies the motor cortex to a specific voluntary movement in the scenario.

sample_responses.response: Gavin uses more of his motor cortex to use his fingers to draw the picture.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly connects the motor cortex to the fine motor movement of drawing.

sample_responses.response: Gavin moves a lot when he plays.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes movement generally without indicating the motor cortex's role in controlling those movements.

parts.prompt: Explain how Cognitive map relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin formed a mental picture/layout/orientation/representation of the museum or another location related to the scenario.

required_elements: Gavin develops a mental map of the museum so he can find exhibits on his own.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin develops a mental map of the museum so he can find exhibits on his own.

required_elements: After exploring the museum, Gavin formed a cognitive map allowing him to navigate back to exhibits he enjoyed.
alternatives.correct_example: After exploring the museum, Gavin formed a cognitive map allowing him to navigate back to exhibits he enjoyed.

wrong_examples: Gavin remembered where the entrance was to the museum because of his mental map.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin formed a mental picture, layout, orientation, or representation of the museum. Responses that describe Gavin simply navigating around the museum (without referencing the mental representation) do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin remembered where the entrance was to the museum because of his mental map.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies cognitive map — a mental representation of the museum's spatial layout helps Gavin remember where things are.

sample_responses.response: Gavin has created a mental picture of the museum to help him draw an accurate picture of the layout.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies cognitive map — Gavin's mental picture of the museum's layout supports his later drawing.

sample_responses.response: Gavin creates a cognitive map that helps him navigate around the museum.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes navigation without specifying what a cognitive map is (a mental representation/picture of the layout).

parts.prompt: Explain how Cones in the retina relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Gavin's cones allow him to see color and/or detail (e.g., acuity, clarity, sharpness of vision).

required_elements: Gavin is able to create his picture because the cones in his retina enable him to see color and detail.
alternatives.correct_example: Gavin is able to create his picture because the cones in his retina enable him to see color and detail.

wrong_examples: Gavin uses his cones to see well.
Gavin can see movement out of the corner of his eye.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Gavin's cones allow him to see color and/or detail (acuity, clarity, sharpness of vision). Vague responses (e.g., 'see well') without specifying color or detail, and responses describing peripheral motion detection (a rod function), do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Gavin is able to create his picture because the cones in his retina enable him to see color and detail.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly applies cones — identifying their role in color vision and visual acuity in the context of Gavin drawing a colorful picture.

sample_responses.response: Gavin uses his cones to see well.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; too vague — does not specify color vision or visual acuity as the function of cones.

sample_responses.response: Gavin can see movement out of the corner of his eye.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; peripheral motion detection is a function of rods, not cones.
